summary: In 'The Gospel of Wealth', Andrew Carnegie argues that the self-made rich have a moral obligation to distribute their surplus wealth during their lifetimes to benefit society. He advocates for modest personal living, heavy estate taxes to discourage hoarding, and philanthropic investments that empower people to improve themselves rather than providing direct charity that encourages dependency.

principles.statement: The wealthy must administer their surplus wealth responsibly during their lifetimes to benefit society.
principles.rationale: Surplus wealth produces the greatest net benefit to society when it is carefully administered by the wealthy themselves, rather than being left to heirs who might squander it or left to the state upon death.

principles.statement: Philanthropy should empower individuals to improve themselves rather than fostering dependency.
principles.rationale: Direct handouts can encourage sloth and unworthiness; true charity provides the infrastructure and means for self-betterment so the gift generates even greater wealth for the community.
principles.source_quote: Help those who will help themselves, to provide part of the means by which those who desire to improve may do so.

principles.statement: The state should heavily tax the estates of the wealthy upon their death.
principles.rationale: Hoarding wealth until death is a selfish act. Progressive taxation ensures that excess wealth is returned to the public and condemns the hoarding of capital.
principles.source_quote: By taxing estates heavily at death the State marks its condemnation of the selfish millionaire's unworthy life. It is desirable that nations should go much further in this direction.

frameworks.name: Sustainable Philanthropic Endowment
frameworks.when_to_apply: When funding public institutions (like libraries) to ensure long-term viability and community investment.
steps: The wealthy benefactor provides the initial grant for building and equipping the facility.
Require the municipality to pass an ordinance establishing a tax to support ongoing operating costs.
Establish an independent board of trustees and endowment to manage the organization.

mental_models.name: Wealth as a Public Trust
mental_models.description: Viewing surplus personal wealth not as private property to be hoarded or passed to heirs, but as a trust fund to be administered by the possessor for the benefit of the community during their lifetime.
mental_models.example: Carnegie limiting his personal wealth at age 35 and donating the surplus to build public baths and libraries for the community.

heuristics: Help those who will help themselves.
Live a modest lifestyle and avoid ostentatious extravagance.

signature_quotes.text: By taxing estates heavily at death the State marks its condemnation of the selfish millionaire's unworthy life.
signature_quotes.context: Praising high British taxes on the estates of dead millionaires.

signature_quotes.text: Help those who will help themselves, to provide part of the means by which those who desire to improve may do so.
signature_quotes.context: Clarifying his doctrine to critics who thought he was advocating for free handouts to the poor.

signature_quotes.text: Indeed, it is difficult to set bounds to the share of a rich man's estates which should go at his death to the public through the agency of the State...
signature_quotes.context: Advocating for progressive estate taxes on wealth held until death.

anti_patterns.description: Bequeathing vast fortunes to heirs (patrimony or primogeniture).
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Heirs frequently squander large fortunes in 'riotous living' rather than nurturing and growing them or using them for the public good.

anti_patterns.description: Charitable giving that keeps people in poverty (unconditional handouts).
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It encourages 'the slothful, the drunken, the unworthy' instead of creating opportunities for beneficiaries to better themselves.

anti_patterns.description: Ostentatious living and amassing private treasures.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It is a wasteful use of capital that should instead be administered toward reducing the stratification between the rich and the poor.
